title: Data in rows and columns
checkLabels.t9: The first price is in the table
checkLabels.t11: The second price is in the table
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the data too.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
hint2.untouched: The three paragraphs become a table. Same words, different wrapper.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. In tables it is easy to lose a .
hint2.not-closed: Every cell wants a . Every row wants a . The opening and closing counts must match.
hint3.not-closed: They close from the inside out: before , before .
why.lost-h1: You deleted the page heading. The task was about the three paragraphs, not about it.
why.no-table: You are still lining things up with spaces. Run it and look — the columns are gone. The browser swallows spaces, you know this from lesson 18.
hint2.no-table: Spaces are not a layout tool. Data in rows and columns has a tag of its own.
hint4.no-table: Wrap the three rows of data in a <table>. Then work inside it.
why.no-tr: There are not three rows. The table has a wrapper, but the rows are missing or fewer than three. Each row gets wrapped in <tr>.
hint2.no-tr: Three paragraphs, three rows. The column names are a row too — they are the first one.
hint3.no-tr:
A similar case: ......
. Rows are children of the table.
why.no-td: There are not nine cells. Three rows with three things in each — that is nine. Each thing in the row gets wrapped in <td>.
hint2.no-td: Look at one row: Mountain, 18 leva, 26 inches. Three separate things. Three cells.
hint3.no-td: A similar case: MondayRain12. Three cells in one row.
hint4.no-td: td comes from table data. Every value gets a cell of its own.
why.empty-td: There is an empty cell. An empty cell is not data — it is a hole. Check that every value landed inside its own <td>.
hint2.empty-td: This often comes from Mountain — the value ended up outside the cell, between it and the next one.
why.lost-data: You lost data. Both bikes, their prices and their sizes — all of it belongs in the table. The task was to move it, not to trim it.
why.lost-head: The column names are gone. Model, Price, Wheels — they are the first row. Do not delete them, even if they look redundant. The next lesson is about exactly them.
why.still-p: The old paragraphs are still there. You built the table but kept the previous version — the data now shows twice.
hint2.still-p: Delete the three paragraphs with the spaces. The <h1> stays.
blocks.0.text: You are selling two bikes and you want to compare them — model, price, wheel size.
blocks.1.text: You lined them up with spaces, like on a typewriter. Run it.
blocks.2.text: They scattered. The columns are gone. The browser swallows your spaces — you know this from lesson 18. There is no fooling it here.
blocks.5.text: <table> is the table itself. <tr> is one row — from table row. <td> is one cell — from table data.
blocks.6.text: You write row by row, left to right. Exactly as you read.
blocks.8.text: Notice what is missing: there is no tag for a column. There is no <tc>.
blocks.9.text: Columns happen by themselves. If every row has three cells — there are three columns. If one row has four and the others three — the table breaks and you see it immediately.
blocks.10.text: You write rows. Columns are a consequence.
blocks.12.text: Run it and look. No lines, no borders, no separators. The cells are glued together and barely readable.
blocks.14.text: The table is for the data. The lines are for the eyes. And eyes are a job for CSS.
blocks.15.text: Every beautiful table you have ever seen is exactly this underneath: <tr> and <td>. Nothing more.
blocks.17.text: Turn the three paragraphs into a real table. Three rows, three columns. And when you are done — delete the paragraphs. Do not leave the data in two places.
blocks.18.text: Run it and compare: the spaces vanished, the columns stayed.
blocks.19.text: But something is off. The first row — Model, Price, Wheels — is not data. It is the names of the columns.
blocks.20.text: And you told it <td>. Meaning: just another data cell. The next lesson is about that.
